sentences.text: Yeah.
I-- danger every day-- every day-- when-- we had no right at all.
The-- everybody could kill us.
A person didn't have no right.
A little guy could kill me-- any-- any kid-- not a Jew-- Jews.
They could do anything to you.
He didn't have no punishment for that, because Jews didn't have rights-- no right at all.
The danger was very hard to go out on the streets.
You have to hide yourself as a woman and as a human being.
Soldiers, they used to-- used to get in in the houses and look for woman.
I had to hide myself-- that's a-- from the soldiers, too.
Yeah.
I didn't experience this, but I heard about it.
A friend of mine was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --the soldiers came in in the house.
And she was a girl-- a young girl.
So they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --molested her.
She told us.
I heard about it.
And I heard about-- the thing.
And I was hiding the minute I heard some soldiers was going on the freeway, I went on the attic there-- my sister too.
Both of us went on the attic and hide.
Then if you want to go for food, you have to go in-- in danger.
You cannot-- you cannot get food either.
You afraid.
Actually, the real SS people didn't-- they didn't want to have that to do nothing with Jewish girls.
But the another soldier-- there was plain soldiers, so they-- not all the time-- not all the time.
You know, we have to be careful-- had to be careful as a woman and as a-- as a human being to both things.
And a man had the same problem.
A man has a problem as a man.
He has to go-- and my father use to came in and there are soldiers.
I-- my mother saw it.
And they looked.
And-- and my father was a orthodox Jew-- didn't wear no hair on the-- on the head.
That was with a-- with a-- you know, without the hair at all.
And he weared a little kippah of a yamaka and a hat.
So they took off-- they looked on his head.
They thought he's a-- he's a soldier.
Because the Russian soldier, there is-- they are all bald.
They-- they cut him up to-- you know, they cut him up-- the hair.
So they thought he's a-- are you a soldier-- a soldier?
And already, they taking out their weapons and want to kill him.
He says, I'm a old man.
My father was, that time, 52 years old-- 50-- 50 or 51, I think.
So finally, my mother start begging them and talking to them.
He's a old man!
He's nothing.
He not wearing because-- he doesn't have hair because it is-- that's the way he grows there.
He don't have a haircut.
The soldiers, they don't have haircuts-- the Russians.
So he thought he's a soldier.
He wants to kill him.
So my mother saved my father that way.
Yeah.
Yeah?
It changed in-- especially only with-- in education.
I start to go to school a lot.
But religious things was very hard.
They want to bring me in in the-- in their organization, you know, the communist organization-- the Komsomol, they called-- the youth-- youth organization.
But I didn't go in, because against my religious beliefs.
I didn't want.
I'm not a communist!
They want to-- they used to tell me to go in.
I says, no.
I'm not-- I'm not the type.
Well, I go, I'm not against the Russian, but I'm not a communist.
I don't want to be a-- in the communist youth.
So some, they didn't went in.
My husband-- my father was very, very pleased that I said I don't want to go in in the youth.
He was very pleased, because he didn't like the communists too much.
In fact, my father-- they-- he was a businessman.
So he couldn't go to work something else.
You know, what he-- he was doing as a-- as a janitor in a office.
That's the only work he could get-- as the janitor.
Russian occupation-- he was a janitor.
That's all what he was working.
But the only benefit I had, I went to school in that office what he was a janitor.
The president from-- from that office liked my father.
My father was a very likable person-- a good person.
He said, I want to send your daughter.
I see she's a nice girl.
I'll send her to-- I'll give her a-- a break, and I'll send her to school to the big town.
So that guy had good relations with my father.
And my father begged him.
Send him to school.
That's why I went to school-- according to that guy-- on account of that guy.
He send me, because he liked my father, and he said my daughter.
He knew that he is religious.
Because my father, for the Russians, was janitor.
And janitor, you have to come in early in the morning and put on the stove, you know.
But Saturday, my father didn't put up the stove there-- the wood.
He used to bring in a gentile.
So the guy knew that.
My father used to put on the tefillin in the office.
But nobody knew that.
Nobody was there in the early morning.
He used to tell me too.
I said-- he said he used to tell him.
To me, I don't care.
You can-- you can pray all day long.
But nobody should see you.
I wouldn't say to nobody.
So my father used to put up his tefillin every morning.
And Saturday, he didn't light up the stove.
He had somebody had to light it up for him.
That was the experience.
And my mother, we kept Shabbas like-- like it used to be in growing up-- believe Shabbas.
It was keeping up the tradition for the time from Russia occupation too.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How did things change when the occupation--
The Germans, they had came in there.
It was different.
That time, you couldn't-- you had to do everything-- whatever they tell you.
We was glad we had some wood to make a stove.
We didn't look at a Shabbas, or it's a-- it's a Yontif-- nothing.
There was nothing.
You had to do everything.
That my mother says, [NON-ENGLISH] they called.
In a-- in emergency case, you can do everything.
It's emergency.
And my grandmother was very, very religious, too.
She says, I cannot understand that comes in a new-- a new-- and what do you call it?
She called it a new king.
She called it a king.
And you should do such a things for Jews?
She knew that too.
I cannot understand.
I went through World War II.
It wasn't so bad.
World War I.
Yeah, World War I-- excuse me.
It wasn't so bad.
Nobody touched nobody.
And this, she-- she said, I cannot understand.
A king who brings such a things and people.
My mother's mother was living with us.
And she died that year.
She couldn't understand it.
Nobody couldn't understand.
Brought to--
--the Germans--
--or the Russians?
Because she said, they didn't have nothing to eat.
And they don't-- they give all kind of rules against the Jews.
I mean, I cannot understand.
She says, what kind of-- what kind of people is this?
The German does in World War II by us too, and we didn't have this experience.
How come you haven't-- how come they're-- they couldn't-- she couldn't understand it's a different system.
That was a system from Hitler.
They wants to-- Hitler wants to kill all the Jews.
We start at the beginning, right away.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's what they experienced.
That what she tell us-- told us.
We-- we thought she's a older lady.
She doesn't understand.
She didn't understand-- no papers, no books, no letters.
During the occupation from Germany, you didn't have no connection with the world.
Nothing came in to you.
The only thing what you had the communication is through Ukrainian better people.
For whatever, they came in from another town, or villages, or-- or they came in and they told you what's going on.
And otherwise, you didn't have no papers-- nothing-- no schooling for Jews-- nothing!
My father and mother what?
The work?
Men-- they-- they confiscated.
They didn't have no work.
They didn't work.
They dragged him-- my father-- they dragged to work on a-- they put in a cable telephone.
My father was a very skinny guy, you know, a Jewish man-- a tall man.
He was around-- I think my father was about 5' 8', 5' 9'-- tall, skinny.
They dragged him on the-- to put on the cable and work on the cable to-- on his shoulders.
It was pretty hard, but he worked.
And they schlepped him there.
They-- they took him there to work, and he was working every day.
He got had plenty, plenty knocks from the Germans to drag there in the heavy cable on-- on his shoulders-- telephone to the front.
They build a telephone.
And this was a highway.
And he was working there.
Any work-- he did all kind of work.
They took him there.
But, we tried to protect him.
They called up sometimes and they want him to go to work.
And we used to hide him and attempt-- the same place there.
He's not home.
There was Jewish police too in the ghetto-- Jewish police.
They used to-- they had to have some people to work.
They used to come in and drag some people out-- men, young girls, young boys.
But sometimes they came in in the house, and they asked me.
I said, my father's not home.
He's working.
He's not home.
Yeah, he was hiding under there.
In fact, this-- there was a time when they was looking for men.
And they came.
They came in.
They was looking.
They're knocking over the whole house.
They couldn't find him.
We-- we hided him up-- hide him down the space.
And he was laying there.
And nobody dragged him.
The only thing, he was killed in this-- on the end, in the ghetto-- you know, the liquidation from the ghetto.
That time, he was killed.
All the time, we tried to protect him-- not to let him to go out-- sit him in the house and not to let him out to work, unless they grabbed him someplace to go to work.
Because he was very you know, a religious man.
Try to-- my mother used to put up her shoulders not to let him out no place.
My mother was a very, very nice woman-- smart woman-- very smart woman.
But in the end, she's-- my father said to my mother, I don't know if I'm going to be alive-- but to go and save the children-- try to save the children.
So after the war, the times we had, because he went with us-- we ran-- we ran away from the ghetto.
Shall I start it already?
Shall I start from the ghetto?
Yes, please.
Tell me about the ghetto and ghetto life, and then before the liquidation-- or the liquidation came before?
In-- yeah.
In the ghetto life is-- it was every day.
We knew already that-- that the days are short by us.
I understood.
My mother, my sister understood.
My sister was working by German store.
I was working too, every day.
I went one time.
I want to buy something in a-- by a-- by Ukrainian woman.
She sold something.
So the Ukrainian woman went into the ghetto.
Went in-- in the ghetto to sell something.
They want to make some money.
So I told the woman-- and I tried to hide her.
The German police was looking around for her.
I hide her in my place, because I want to buy something for her-- food.
So the German came in, and he dragged out the woman.
Then-- and he told her why she didn't want-- they didn't want the-- the Ukrainian to come in and to sell, you know, goods for the Jews.
And then he start beating me up, because I hide her to get the goods.
They beat me up-- black and blue.
Wanted to-- went in-- they want to buy something from her.
So that's what I had a-- an experience-- this experience.
And so every day was something different.
There was Germans in the ghetto going there.
There was one guy.
We had a-- a guy, he was-- that was killed near the ghetto that had-- the Jews-- that he was a gentile guy, and the Jews killed him.
And then it came out that he was killed on the freeway-- the highway there-- on the freeway.
A machine-- a auto machine killed him.
Finally, they didn't say about the Jews.
But they want to make it up that the Jews killed him.
And that was every day like that up to September-- the-- the liquidation of the ghetto.
The day when the-- when we find out that they are-- want to-- they want to liquidate as a whole, my mother said the only way, we have to-- we have to dress ourself up like-- like peasant girls.
And we tried to go out from the ghetto.
So we dressed ourself like peasant girls.
And we went out on the main-- main-- main street-- the main street we went out-- out from the ghetto.
Nobody touched us that time.
But the minute we went out onto the-- onto the town, a policeman stopped us.
And we had some things with us-- a little-- a few things to, you know, to have something-- to buy some food-- you-- money, a little bit-- marks.
The-- the policeman said, I know who you are.
And where are you going now?
You go back in the ghetto.
My sister, she was so-- she start kissing him in the legs, and in the hand, in the face.
Please, let us go.
Let us go.
I was stoned like a stone.
I couldn't move from the place.
I got like-- I was paralyzed.
He says, go back.
I was paralyzed-- practically paralyzed.
But my sister already had-- she start kissing him and begging him.
And he said, you don't need nothing, nothing-- and took away everything-- whatever we had.
And she was begging him, and kissing him.
So finally, he let us go-- let us go somewhere.
We went-- father there.
My mother had a-- a woman.
They knew some people-- what she was dealing with them in business.
She went in to the-- to the people there.
It was around five, six kilometer from the town.
So we slept over the night.
And the woman says, we-- I cannot keep you here, because over there on the city, they're going to look for you.
Somebody neighbors saw you that you went into my place.
You have to go.
So there-- I said-- my mother said, well now it's in the morning.
Let us stay till the evening.
And then we will go away.
I was staying until the evening.
And in the evening, we start marching for 30 kilometer deep into the Russian-- the Russian occupied Germany.
That was Russia there already-- in the place where my mother was born.
And the place was named-- it's a village, [NON-ENGLISH].
My mother was born there.
So it took us all night and all day.
During the day, we was hiding in the bushes.
At night, we was walking-- you know, walking.
The night, all night, we walked.
We didn't-- we had to look.
My sister was a little one.
So we took her.
I took her on my shoulders.
My mother, she looked on the-- on the signs how to go, where to go.
She looked up, and over there it was dark.
They don't have lights.
They don't have nothing.
So we-- this way, we find our way to go to that village.
And we came in on a Sunday morning in that village.
And my mother knew-- she was born there.
She knew all the houses there and all the people-- all the people.
She went on the end of the village there and ran into a woman.
She knew the people there where they lived.
And that was a-- already a daughter.
That was not the older people.
Her daughter was there.
And she was asking for bread.
We was hungry.
People be hungry.
You need to eat!
You're a human being.
You have to eat something-- asking for bread and water.
Then a woman-- and she told her who she are-- is.
The little guys already knew that this is-- this is already a [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH], this is in Russian-- in Ukrainian.
It means Jew.
The little-- little-- little kids, they put in in their eyes that Jews are not people.
And the little kids went away right away-- the little kids, like a 10-year-old, or 9, 10, 11-year-old kids.
They went right away to the [NON-ENGLISH] over there to the city village.
And they said that the Jews are in the village.
And there was Germans there collecting some duties-- were about three, four Germans.
And that woman-- girl-- was there in the village-- the girl, Nadia Storazuk.
I told you about it.
And she ran back.
They knew where my mother and we were stationed there, by that woman.
And she ran down barefooted.
She was my age-- about 18-years-old-- 17-- about that.
And she came here-- there, and she says, listen!
Germans are looking already for you.
You have to go out from the house.
Go here in the woods.
There is a woods-- wood around the village.
My mother says, ah, nobody will touch me.
I'm here in my-- in my-- in my village.
We heard that-- that she said that in the woods.
We ran away.
My sister and myself, we ran out from the house right away-- and start running, and find themselves a bush.
And it was not far from the woods-- in-- hiding under the bush.
My mother wants to already see.
She lost us already.
She wants to see.
So the girl says, I know where they went.
Come on.
I'll show you.
Go.
Go run to the woods.
Take the girls, and run to the woods.
We was sitting there, under the bush.
And the minute my mother sit down there, the Germans was go looking for us all over there in the-- in the same place.
And they didn't see us into the-- under the bush.
They didn't see.
So they went back to the city, over there-- whatever it is.
And we was left there till the evening in that bush.
But it was not a big bush.
In the evening, you want to eat something.
You need-- you need food.
We start to go on farther.
And my-- my mother knew that there was another little village.
My grandfather used to own the-- he had a farm over there-- my mother's grandfather, my great grandfather.
And she knew-- she knew all the-- the-- the places there.
She growed up there.
She went in the house there on a corner.
And she was asking for a piece of bread-- food.
We was still there on the bush.
The woman saw us.
She got so scared.
She says, oh my God!
What are you doing here?
They are looking for people like you!
The Germans are looking for you.
Out!
Out, out of the house!
So, my mother went out.
She didn't realize that over there was sitting another guy, a neighbor.
And he recognized my mother.
He ran out, after my mother.
He says-- my mother's name was Bella.
He said, Bella, come in here.
I want to talk to you.
Oh!
She got so-- she recognized him right away.
That was Ivan-- Ivan Chaichuk-- the neighbor.
He says, go there in the woods.
He told her the place.
She knew where to go.
Sit there, and I come-- I'll come and I'll bring you food, and water, whatever you need.
So, it was already a little dark.
We went there to that place where-- in the woods.
About 10 or 11 o'clock at night, he came up with hot milk, bread, butter, potatoes.
Oh my God.
It was like-- like from heaven-- God send us.
I eat up.
And he was sitting and talking up to 1-- 12, 1 o'clock with my mother.
He said, listen Bella.
I like to save you and your children like my own.
But now is bad, because they know you around here.
You have to hide yourself around the village that's here on the woods.
And if it's going to quiet down, I'll see what I can do for you.
So my mother, we was sitting.
He was-- that was fall-- late in the-- it was September already-- the middle of September-- late in September.
What year?
Night-- it was cold already-- so wherever.
We took some sleeping in the-- in the bushes.
We-- we covered ourself with leaves, with the-- with straw.
It was cold already.
Every night, he used to come in the same place and bring us food.
And then it quiet down.
And he start talking about to give us a place where to hide.
So he said there is one guy who is a very nice person-- Tedoch Storazuk.
Hey, I'll go to him.
And I'll talk to him about you.
He maybe-- you cannot be together.
You have to split yourself.
You have to go separately.
So maybe he will take one person-- but keep alive.
My mother said, OK, anything is good for me, whatever you say.
So, the next day-- it was a couple of weeks.
It was really cold already.
It was a couple weeks.
It was about three, four weeks in the woods-- no-- no-- no clothes-- no change in clothes, no washing-- just a little bit-- and in the evening, wherever-- he brought us some water.
So he said, yes.
OK, I went to the-- to Mr.
Storazuk, to the guy.
He says he's willing to take one person.
Oh, my mother teared up.
My sister had a-- injured her foot a little bit.
She says, well, you-- maybe you'll go, because you have a injured foot from walking.
She says, no, ma, I'm not going.
I stay with you.
I don't want to go no place.
I stay with you-- very small-- a little one.
She wants to keep-- they was- she wants to stay with mother, you know, kids.
She was-- how old was she? 16 years old-- 15-- 15 years old.
No, I'm not going no place.
I stay with you.
Whatever-- it's going to be with you, I'll-- I'll be with you.
So, I says, I don't have no choice.
I have to go.
So he took me, that guy.
And he brought me into that-- Storazuk-- Tedoch Storazuk.
And that was his daughter-- is Nadia.
He had one daughter-- that's only-- the son and a daughter.
The son was mobilizing the Red Army.
And he didn't-- he didn't know where he is.
So, I was there.
And that-- and they give me food.
They kept me hiding on the attic in the straw.
They had straw a lot in the village.
And I hided there.
And it's a barn-- and a big barn.
On the barn on top is a lot of straw.
They keep it for the-- for winter.
They keep the straw-- straw, hay.
For the animal, he had some cows, and sheeps, and chickens-- well, like on a farm.
I was in there.
And every evening she-- twice a day to-- he would bring me food in the evening-- in the morning and in the evening-- soup, bread, everything.
Whatever they had, they give me food.
One day, they had a big dog.
The dog was very, very-- were-- on a chain-- barking terrible.
[NON-ENGLISH].
He barked like anything.
One day, my mother didn't have no place where to go.
She came up in-- on the ladder-- by the ladder.
And the dog didn't even give one bark.
And she went up on the ladder, and she came up to me.
And she said, I have to stay here.
I-- I-- Rachel-- I put her by another man or a lady for a while.
I don't know how long, but I don't have where to be-- go.
So I came up here, and we stay with the other.
He should know nothing about it.
So I was happy to see my mother.
And I hided her deep in the straw in the hay.
The-- the guy shouldn't know-- the dog shouldn't know that she's here.
Whatever he give me food, I split with my mother.
I eat with my mother.
So that took about couple days.
In a couple days, my sister came to the-- Tedoch.
The other guy chased her out.
He said he doesn't want to keep her no longer.
She came to-- she know where we are.
She came to that guy and-- to Mr.
Tedoch-- Tedoch-- Rachel.
And she want to see me.
She didn't know that my mother-- that mother was there.
So he brought her over up to me on the evening.
And he says, I cannot keep you-- both of you.
If you want to stay both, you-- you have to-- you better go out-- both of you from here-- only one-- one person-- that's it.
We'll-- we'll pick up in a moment.
OK.
